name: pysheds (Watershed Delineation in Python)
pricing_notes: Open source (GPL-3.0); Matt Bartos / UT Austin

performance.notes: NumPy-backed; fast for DEM sizes up to ~30M cells

agent_readiness.notes: Python library for watershed delineation from DEMs. from pysheds.grid import Grid. grid = Grid.from_raster('dem.tif'). dem = grid.read_raster('dem.tif'). pit_filled = grid.fill_pits(dem). flooded = grid.fill_depressions(pit_filled). inflated = grid.resolve_flats(flooded). fdir = grid.flowdir(inflated). acc = grid.accumulation(fdir). branches = grid.extract_river_network(fdir, acc > 1000). x, y = -97.294, 32.737. catch = grid.catchment(x=x, y=y, fdir=fdir). Exports to raster. Returns numpy/grid objects.

gotchas: GPL-3.0 license — copyleft implications for commercial use
Only for DEMs — terrain/hydrological use case only
Large DEMs (>100M cells) may require chunked processing
Coordinate system handling can be tricky
best_when: Watershed delineation and catchment area calculation from DEMs
River network extraction and flow accumulation
Hydrological analysis for NHD (National Hydrography Dataset) workflows
When a pure-Python, numpy-backed hydrology tool is needed
avoid_when: Complex terrain analysis beyond hydrology (use WhiteboxTools)
Very large DEMs (>100M cells) without chunking
summary: pysheds provides watershed delineation and hydrological analysis from DEMs. Grid.from_raster('dem.tif'). fill_pits → fill_depressions → resolve_flats → flowdir → accumulation → catchment. extract_river_network(fdir, acc > 1000). GPL-3.0 by Matt Bartos. Best pure-Python tool for watershed delineation. GPL copyleft for commercial use. Handles DEMs up to ~30M cells efficiently.
